questions.question: What is the difference between PUT and PATCH?
questions.answer: PUT replaces the entire resource. If your user has {name, email, age} and you PUT {name: "Alice"}, the result is {name: "Alice"} — email and age are gone. PATCH updates only the specified fields. PATCH {name: "Alice"} → {name: "Alice", email: "old@example.com", age: 30} — other fields preserved. Use PUT when you always send the complete resource; use PATCH for partial updates (more common in practice).

questions.question: What makes an API "RESTful"?
questions.answer: REST has 6 constraints: (1) Client-Server separation — client and server evolve independently. (2) Stateless — each request contains all information needed; server stores no session. (3) Cacheable — responses indicate whether they can be cached. (4) Uniform Interface — standard HTTP methods and URL patterns. (5) Layered System — client doesn't know if it's talking to a proxy, load balancer, or origin server. (6) Code on Demand (optional) — server can send executable code (JavaScript). Most APIs that call themselves "REST" only partially implement these constraints.

questions.question: What is the difference between authentication and authorization?
questions.answer: Authentication: verifying IDENTITY — "who are you?" (login, JWT, OAuth). Authorization: verifying PERMISSIONS — "what are you allowed to do?" (RBAC, ACL). Example: You authenticate with your GitHub credentials (you are @alice). Then GitHub authorizes: you can push to your repos (admin), read public repos (read), but cannot push to other users' private repos (access denied). HTTP status codes: 401 Unauthorized = not authenticated. 403 Forbidden = authenticated but not authorized.

questions.question: What are the performance characteristics of high-frequency Network POST Requests queries?
questions.answer: It depends on the data structure used. Average queries execute in O(1) time when using hash indexes, but can degrade to O(N) linear search if colliding.
